text.title: Stakeholder Engagement Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the stakeholder-engagement methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Apply the default cadence by quadrant: Manage Closely=weekly 1:1, Keep Satisfied=biweekly summary, Keep Informed=monthly digest, Monitor=release notes only.
rule.rationale: Default cadence is empirically calibrated; deviating without rationale causes fatigue (over-engagement) or surprises (under-engagement).

rule.statement: Require an evidence field on every attitude assertion — no evidence means attitude='unknown', not 'supportive'.
rule.rationale: LLMs over-classify everyone as supportive (politeness bias); forcing evidence eliminates false positives.

rule.statement: Triangulate self-reported attitude with at least one behavioural signal: meeting attendance, response time, escalations, or sentiment.
rule.rationale: People say 'supportive' in workshops and undermine in private; behaviour is less gameable.

rule.statement: Always include a hidden stakeholder pass: legal, infosec, procurement, works council/union, accessibility, downstream API consumers.
rule.rationale: Systematically missed stakeholders are the top source of late-stage blockers; they never appear in org-chart exports.

rule.statement: Store the register in git (YAML), not a wiki — diffs become the engagement history; CODEOWNERS gate sponsor sign-off on attitude changes.
rule.rationale: Wiki pages have no diff, no per-field access control, no CI integration.

rule.statement: Engagement communications to executives, regulators, customers, board are drafted by the agent and sent by the human.
rule.rationale: Auto-send is forbidden — tone and timing decisions are governance acts.

list.item: Solo founders or very small teams (under 5 stakeholders)
One-off internal hotfixes or refactors with no business stakeholder change
Crisis or incident response — incident command structure replaces engagement plan during P0
